claim: The C-type lectin receptor (CLEC-2) rearranges the actin cytoskeleton in dendritic cells to suppress motility along stromal surfaces.

result.claim: The C-type lectin receptor (CLEC-2) rearranges the actin cytoskeleton in dendritic cells to suppress motility along stromal surfaces.
result.reasoning: The claim states that CLEC-2 rearranges the actin cytoskeleton in dendritic cells to *suppress* motility along stromal surfaces. However, evidence from paper ID 7370282, "Podoplanin-Rich Stromal Networks Induce Dendritic Cell Motility via Activation of the C-type Lectin Receptor CLEC-2", directly refutes this. The abstract of this paper explicitly states: "Thus, activation of CLEC-2 by PDPN rearranges the actin cytoskeleton in DCs to *promote* efficient motility along stromal surfaces." This is further supported by proposition 1 from the same paper: "Activation of CLEC-2 by PDPN rearranges the actin cytoskeleton in DCs." and proposition 15: "Dendritic cells migrate along stromal scaffolds that display the glycoprotein podoplanin." The paper clearly indicates that CLEC-2 activation *enhances* dendritic cell motility, rather than suppressing it.

evidence.text: CLEC-2 deficiency in dendritic cells impaired trafficking to lymph nodes.
evidence.source: Podoplanin-Rich Stromal Networks Induce Dendritic Cell Motility via Activation of the C-type Lectin Receptor CLEC-2

evidence.text: Cav-1 functions as a tumor suppressor in the stromal microenvironment.
evidence.source: An Absence of Stromal Caveolin-1 Expression Predicts Early Tumor Recurrence and Poor Clinical Outcome in Human Breast Cancers

evidence.text: Inhibition of the Rho/Rock pathway caused the disappearance of F-actin at midzone.
